Productivity Boost: Automate Google Forms, Sheets, and Calendar With GAS

Google Forms and Google Calendar are two tools most of us use a lot, both for work and for life. This post is about using GAS (Google Apps Script) to integrate them — let's get more done with less manual copying.
Quick context on what we're building.

Say you're an office admin. You hand out a Google Form for your manager to fill in, and then for every submission, you have to manually go to Google Calendar and create an event based on the form data.
This post solves that. The use case as a user story:
User submits data through a Google Form
Submitted data auto-writes into a Google Sheet
Every 10 minutes, the script reads new rows and creates corresponding Google Calendar events
I'm assuming the Google account part is covered — pretty much everyone has one. (You do, right?) If not, register one — otherwise you can't follow along.
We'll create a test calendar so we don't mess up your main one. Open Google Calendar:
on the left to add a calendar:
Fill in the name, description, and time zone (usually auto-picked). Click "Create calendar":
The new calendar shows up in the left sidebar:
We'll need this calendar's ID later, so click it and scroll down to "Integrate calendar" — copy the "Calendar ID":
Calendar IDs look something like this:
(Don't worry — that's just an example, not a real ID.)
With the ID copied, on to the form.
You'll see the initial blank form — customize as needed:
I've set mine up like this for demonstration — feel free to use it as reference:
Now link the form to a sheet. Click "Responses" at the top, then "Link to Sheets":
It asks whether to create a new sheet or link to an existing one — pick "Create a new spreadsheet" and give it a meaningful name so you can find it later:
Google should pop the sheet open for you:
If not, you can also get to it via "Responses" → "View in Sheets":
Form and sheet are ready — onto the code.

GAS stands for Google Apps Script. Basically a Google-hosted JavaScript runtime with first-party access to Google's APIs — so anything you'd want to automate across Google products, GAS is the obvious tool.
If you're already a frontend dev you'll feel right at home. If you're not from a coding background, I'll do my best with detailed screenshots and step-by-step instructions.
To create a GAS project, open the sheet you just linked to your form, click "Extensions" → "Apps Script":
You should land on the GAS editor's initial state:
If not, double-check that you opened Apps Script from the right sheet.
If you don't open Apps Script via the sheet's "Extensions" menu, you'll run into a few issues later. I'll mention them as we go — but the recommendation is always to open GAS from the sheet.
in the function dropdown, then click "Run". You should see "Hello World" appear in the "Execution log" — confirms GAS is working.

, click "Run". A permission prompt pops up — click "Review permissions":
Pick the Google account you're using for GAS — it needs your account to access your sheet:
Then a scary-looking "Google hasn't verified this app" warning — click "Advanced", then "Go to XXX (unsafe)":
Don't panic — this is just Google's general security warning. Since this is your own project, it's safe to proceed. You only see this on first run.
The next dialog tells you the GAS project needs access to your sheet — click "Allow":
You should now see a bunch of empty arrays in the Execution log — that's because the sheet has no data yet:
(If nothing shows up at all, try running once more — sometimes the first run fails due to the permission flow.)
Now go back to the form and submit a row of test data (please use the form — don't manually edit the sheet):
Re-run the GAS function and you should see your submission in the Execution log:

is the GAS file extension — you don't need to include it when renaming.
I'm calling it "pseudo" environment variables because GAS doesn't actually have env vars — but the pattern of putting config in one place is the same idea.
Quick recap on env vars — they're a mechanism for using different config in different environments (e.g., different DB passwords for dev vs. prod) without touching code.
Earlier we copied a Google Calendar ID. To make it easier to maintain (change the ID in one place, not scattered everywhere), we'll put it in a config-style file.

This is a bit dense. All it's doing is parsing time strings stored in the sheet into a format Google Calendar can use.
The regex here matches Traditional Chinese AM/PM markers (
) because that's what Google Sheets renders when your locale is zh-TW. If your locale renders AM/PM in English, you'd want a regex like
instead. The simpler approach in the next section sidesteps the locale issue entirely.

Congrats — sheet data is now landing in your calendar.

Last step — make it run automatically. Click "Triggers" on the left sidebar:
Hit "Save" and you're done. The function fires every 10 minutes from here on.
Submit another form entry, wait 10 minutes, and the new event should show up on your calendar.

GAS can do way more than just Sheet → Calendar — but the goal of this post is just to give you a feel for automating with it. There's a bit of code involved, but for anyone with even light JS exposure it's not bad.

Google Forms and Google Calendar are tools we use constantly, but copying form submissions into the calendar by hand is the kind of busywork that screams "automate me". This post walks through using Google Apps Script (GAS) to wire Forms → Sheets → Calendar end to end — reading sheet data, parsing the locale-dependent time format, avoiding duplicate event creation, and triggering the script on a 10-minute schedule.
